title: Verify etcd encryption-at-rest for Kubernetes Secrets storing Solana keypairs
description: The operator and treasury Solana keypairs are stored as Kubernetes Secrets via the OpenBao pipeline (Task 1, subtask 1003). If etcd encryption-at-rest is not enabled, these high-value signing keys are stored in plaintext on disk. This task adds verification that etcd EncryptionConfiguration is active, ensures the OpenBao integration uses short-lived leases where possible, and confirms CI keypair material is never logged or persisted beyond its immediate use.
subtasks: Create a verification script (scripts/verify-etcd-encryption.sh) that checks the Kubernetes API server for EncryptionConfiguration resource presence and confirms 'aescbc' or 'secretbox' provider is configured for secrets
Add the verification script as a pre-flight check in the Helm chart deployment (infra/solana-dev/) — fail deployment if encryption-at-rest is not verified
Review OpenBao secret lease configuration for cto-billing-operator-keypair and cto-billing-treasury-keypair — document current TTL and recommend dynamic secrets or short-lived leases

Audit CI/CD workflow (Task 10, subtask 10003) for keypair exposure: ensure 'set +x' is used before decoding secrets, /tmp/operator.json is written to tmpfs mount (not persistent disk), and shred is called in a trap handler to ensure cleanup on failure
Add a CI lint step that greps workflow files for patterns that could leak secrets (echo of secret vars, shell tracing around secret decoding)
Document the encryption-at-rest requirements in docs/security/secrets-hardening.md with verification procedures for operators
acceptance_criteria: verify-etcd-encryption.sh script exits 0 when EncryptionConfiguration is present with aescbc or secretbox provider, exits 1 otherwise
Helm chart deployment includes the verification as an init container or pre-install hook that blocks deployment on failure
CI workflow decodes operator keypair with 'set +x' and writes to a tmpfs-backed path, not /tmp on persistent storage
CI workflow includes a trap handler that shreds the keypair file on any exit (success or failure)
No CI workflow step echoes or logs secret material — verified by the CI lint step
docs/security/secrets-hardening.md documents the encryption-at-rest verification procedure and OpenBao lease recommendations

title: Document single-operator keypair risk and implement key rotation foundation
description: The entire billing system relies on a single operator keypair to authorize settle_task, refund_task, pause, and unpause. If compromised, an attacker can drain customer balances, issue fraudulent refunds, or DoS the system. For the hackathon, this is documented as a known risk with mitigations. For production readiness, this task lays the groundwork for multi-sig authority transfer and establishes operational controls to limit blast radius.
subtasks: Create docs/security/operator-key-risk-assessment.md documenting: single point of compromise risk, attack scenarios (settlement drain, fraudulent refund, pause DoS), blast radius analysis with current spending caps as mitigation

Document the recommended production path: on-chain multi-sig authority via Squads Protocol or threshold signature scheme, with a time-lock for high-value operations (settlements above a configurable threshold)

Add an on-chain authority transfer instruction (transfer_authority) to the Anchor program that allows the current operator to transfer authority to a new pubkey — this is the primitive needed for key rotation and future multi-sig migration

Implement the transfer_authority instruction with a two-step pattern: propose_authority_transfer (stores pending_authority) and accept_authority_transfer (new authority signs to confirm) to prevent accidental transfers
Add integration tests for transfer_authority: successful two-step transfer, rejection by wrong signer, old authority loses access after transfer
Update Task 15's quarterly rotation documentation to reference the new transfer_authority instruction and include a runbook for emergency key rotation
acceptance_criteria: docs/security/operator-key-risk-assessment.md exists with attack scenarios, blast radius analysis, and production recommendations
transfer_authority instruction compiles and is present in the IDL with both propose and accept steps
Integration tests pass: two-step authority transfer succeeds, old authority cannot settle/pause after transfer, proposed transfer can be cancelled by current authority
The daily spending cap is documented as the primary blast-radius limiter for the hackathon demo
Emergency key rotation runbook exists in docs/operations/key-rotation.md

title: Harden CI/CD pipeline keypair handling and restrict to self-hosted runners
description: The CI/CD deployment job decodes the operator keypair from a GitHub Actions secret into a file on the runner filesystem. On shared GitHub-hosted runners, /tmp may not be securely wiped between jobs, and shell tracing could leak the keypair to logs. This task hardens the CI pipeline by restricting deployment jobs to self-hosted runners (Task 18's AutoscalingRunnerSet), using tmpfs for secret material, preventing shell tracing leaks, and adding a vault-mediated signing proxy as a future option.
subtasks: Modify the deploy-devnet CI job to target the self-hosted runner label (e.g., runs-on: self-hosted) from the AutoscalingRunnerSet, falling back to a documented manual process if self-hosted runners are not yet available
Update the keypair decoding step to write to a tmpfs mount (/dev/shm/operator.json or a dedicated tmpfs volume) instead of /tmp
Add 'set +x' before any line that handles secret material and 'set -x' after to prevent shell tracing leaks
Implement a bash trap handler (trap cleanup EXIT) that shreds the keypair file on any exit path including failures and signals
Add a CI workflow lint job that scans .github/workflows/*.yml for dangerous patterns: echo of ${{ secrets.* }}, missing 'set +x' around secret handling, /tmp usage for secrets

Document the future vault-mediated signing proxy approach in docs/security/ci-signing-architecture.md as the recommended production pattern (keypair never leaves vault, CI sends unsigned transactions to a signing service)
acceptance_criteria: deploy-devnet job specifies self-hosted runner label or documents the fallback procedure
Keypair is written to /dev/shm/ or equivalent tmpfs mount, verified by examining the workflow step
No shell tracing (set -x) is active during secret decoding — verified by the CI lint job
Trap handler cleans up keypair on all exit paths including SIGTERM — verified by adding a deliberate failure test
CI lint job flags any workflow that echoes secrets or uses /tmp for secret material
docs/security/ci-signing-architecture.md describes the vault signing proxy pattern

title: Implement CiliumNetworkPolicy for Solana validator and billing namespaces
description: The Solana test validator pod exposes RPC (8899) and WebSocket (8900) ports via ClusterIP service with no network policy restrictions. Any pod in the cluster can connect and send transactions, including potentially malicious programs or transaction replays. This task creates CiliumNetworkPolicy resources during initial infrastructure setup (not deferred to production hardening) to restrict access to the validator from only authorized namespaces and pods.
subtasks: Create a CiliumNetworkPolicy manifest in infra/solana-dev/templates/ that restricts ingress to the solana-validator service ports (8899, 8900) to only pods in the cto-billing namespace and CI runner namespace
Create a CiliumNetworkPolicy for the cto-billing namespace that restricts egress to only: the solana-validator service, the Irys/Arweave endpoints, and the Kubernetes API server
Add namespace labels (e.g., app.kubernetes.io/part-of: cto-billing) to both the solana-dev and cto-billing namespaces for policy selector targeting

Test the network policies: deploy a test pod outside the allowed namespaces and verify it cannot connect to the validator RPC port; deploy a pod inside the cto-billing namespace and verify it can connect
Document the network policy architecture in docs/security/network-policies.md with a diagram showing allowed traffic flows
Add the network policy manifests to the Helm chart values.yaml with a toggle (networkPolicies.enabled: true) for environments where Cilium is not available
acceptance_criteria: CiliumNetworkPolicy resources are applied to the solana-dev namespace as part of Task 1 Helm chart deployment
A pod in the cto-billing namespace can reach solana-validator:8899 — verified by curl or solana CLI from within the pod
A pod in a different namespace (e.g., default) cannot reach solana-validator:8899 — connection times out or is refused
Network policies are toggleable via Helm values for environments without Cilium
docs/security/network-policies.md documents the policy architecture and allowed traffic flows

title: Add vault balance invariant testing and define refund funding model
description: The shared USDC vault pools all customer deposits, creating cross-customer fund commingling risk. A bug in settlement logic could allow one customer's settlement to use another customer's funds. Additionally, the refund instruction's funding model is ambiguous — if treasury funds have been withdrawn externally, refunds could fail or drain other customers' funds. This task adds invariant testing to ensure vault solvency and clearly defines the refund funding model.
subtasks: Implement a vault balance invariant test in the integration test suite (Task 8): after every settlement and refund operation, assert that sum(all CustomerBalance.balance) + sum(all settled but unrefunded TaskReceipt.amount) equals the vault token account balance

Create a dedicated invariant test file (tests/08-invariants.test.ts) that runs multi-customer scenarios: 3 customers deposit, multiple settlements with different agent packages, partial refunds — assert vault balance invariant holds after every operation

Define and document the refund funding model: refunds transfer from treasury back to vault; add a require! check in refund_task that treasury token account balance >= refund amount; document that if treasury has insufficient funds, the refund fails gracefully

Add an on-chain invariant check helper function that can be called in settle_task and refund_task (optional for hackathon, recommended for production): after balance mutations, verify customer_balance.balance >= 0 and vault_balance >= customer_balance.balance
Document the per-customer vault approach as a production recommendation in docs/security/vault-architecture.md, explaining the tradeoffs between shared vault (current) and per-customer vaults
Add a stress test that settles tasks for multiple customers concurrently and verifies no cross-customer fund leakage via the invariant check
acceptance_criteria: Invariant test (tests/08-invariants.test.ts) passes with 3+ customers, 5+ settlements, and 2+ refunds — vault balance equals sum of all customer balances at every step
refund_task instruction includes a check that treasury has sufficient balance for the refund amount, failing gracefully with a descriptive error if insufficient
The refund funding model is documented in docs/security/vault-architecture.md with clear description of fund flow: treasury → vault on refund
Multi-customer stress test passes without cross-customer fund leakage
Production recommendation for per-customer vaults is documented with migration path

title: Hardcode USDC mint address and add null-byte validation for PDA string seeds
description: The program does not validate the USDC mint address, potentially allowing an attacker to deposit a worthless token and settle tasks against it. Additionally, PDA string seeds (package_id, task_id) could contain null bytes that cause PDA collisions. This task hardcodes the USDC mint address, adds mint validation constraints to all token instructions, and adds null-byte validation for string-based PDA seeds.
subtasks: Add a USDC mint address constant to the program: for devnet use a configurable constant (set during build or in OperatorConfig during initialization), for mainnet hardcode the canonical USDC mint (EPjFWdd5AufqSSqeM2qN1xzybapC8G4wEGGkZwyTDt1v)
Add `token::mint = usdc_mint` constraints on all token account references in deposit, withdraw, settle_task, and refund_task instruction account structs
Add a validation check in initialize_operator that stores the USDC mint address in OperatorConfig (if not hardcoded) and make it immutable after initialization
Implement a null-byte validation helper function: fn validate_no_null_bytes(input: &str) -> Result<()> that returns an error if the input contains any 0x00 bytes
Call validate_no_null_bytes on package_id in register_agent_package and on task_id in settle_task before PDA derivation

Add test cases: (a) deposit with wrong SPL token mint fails, (b) register_agent_package with null bytes in package_id fails, (c) settle_task with null bytes in task_id fails, (d) normal operations with valid mint and clean strings succeed
acceptance_criteria: USDC mint address is validated in every token instruction — depositing a non-USDC SPL token fails with a descriptive error
OperatorConfig stores the USDC mint address (or it is a program constant) and all instructions reference it
register_agent_package rejects package_id containing null bytes (0x00) with InvalidPackageId error
settle_task rejects task_id containing null bytes with InvalidTaskId error
Integration tests pass for all 4 test cases: wrong mint, null-byte package_id, null-byte task_id, and valid operations
The anchor IDL reflects the updated constraints

title: Document trust assumptions for receipt hash and add operator audit logging
description: The settle_task instruction accepts receipt_hash as an operator-provided argument with no on-chain verification that it corresponds to an actual Arweave upload. Additionally, there is no off-chain audit trail for operator administrative actions (settle, refund, pause, unpause). This task documents the trust assumptions, adds structured security event logging to the controller for all operator actions, and creates alerting recommendations.
subtasks: Create docs/security/trust-assumptions.md documenting: receipt_hash is operator-attested (not cryptographically verified on-chain), the operator can submit arbitrary 32-byte values, customers rely on the Arweave verification mechanism in the dashboard to detect mismatches

Document the production path for receipt verification: oracle-based Arweave data availability verification, or a dispute period where customers can challenge mismatched receipt hashes within N slots of settlement

Add structured security event logging in the controller billing module (Task 5, subtask 5007) for all operator actions: emit structured log entries (JSON format) for settle, refund, pause, unpause events including operator pubkey, transaction signature, amounts, customer affected, and timestamp
Create a log schema definition in docs/security/audit-log-schema.md specifying the exact fields and format for each security event type

Add alerting recommendations for Datadog/monitoring: refund rate exceeding 10% in a 1-hour window, any pause event, settlements above a configurable threshold amount, operator key usage from unexpected IP ranges
Implement a basic anomaly detection check in the controller: if refund count in the last hour exceeds 10% of settlements, log a WARN-level security alert
acceptance_criteria: docs/security/trust-assumptions.md exists documenting the receipt_hash trust model and its limitations
Controller emits structured JSON log entries for every settle, refund, pause, and unpause operation with all required fields (operator pubkey, tx signature, amount, customer, timestamp)
docs/security/audit-log-schema.md defines the schema for each event type with field names, types, and example values
Alerting recommendations are documented with specific thresholds and rationale
The anomaly detection check logs a WARN when refund rate exceeds 10% — verified by unit test with mocked counters

title: Add rate limiting and CSRF considerations for web dashboard
description: The web dashboard is a purely client-side static export with no server-side authentication, session management, or rate limiting on RPC calls. An attacker could abuse the public RPC endpoint through the dashboard's frontend. This task adds client-side rate limiting for Solana RPC calls, documents the authentication model's limitations, and prepares for CDN-layer rate limiting.
subtasks: Implement client-side RPC rate limiting in the dashboard's Solana connection wrapper: limit to 10 RPC calls per second per session using a token bucket algorithm in src/lib/rate-limiter.ts
Add request deduplication for getProgramAccounts calls using TanStack Query's built-in caching — ensure the same query is not fired multiple times in rapid succession

Document the authentication model limitations in docs/security/dashboard-security.md: wallet signature is client-side only, no server-side session validation, no protection against RPC endpoint abuse beyond client-side limits
Add Content Security Policy (CSP) headers in the Next.js config or meta tags: restrict script-src to 'self', connect-src to the configured Solana RPC URL and Arweave gateway only
Document the production recommendation for a backend-for-frontend (BFF) layer that validates wallet signatures server-side, implements session management, and proxies/rate-limits Solana RPC calls
Add Cloudflare CDN rate limiting configuration documentation in docs/deployment/cdn-config.md for the production deployment (rate limit by IP to 100 requests/minute)
acceptance_criteria: Client-side rate limiter prevents more than 10 RPC calls per second — verified by test that rapidly fires 20 calls and confirms throttling
TanStack Query caching prevents duplicate getProgramAccounts calls within the cache TTL window
Content Security Policy headers are set restricting connect-src to Solana RPC and Arweave domains only
docs/security/dashboard-security.md documents the client-only auth model, its limitations, and the production BFF recommendation
docs/deployment/cdn-config.md documents Cloudflare rate limiting configuration

title: Pin dependency versions and add supply chain security scanning to CI
description: Multiple packages use caret ranges for critical dependencies (e.g., @coral-xyz/anchor ^0.30.0, @solana/web3.js ^1.95), which can lead to inconsistencies across environments. This task pins all critical dependencies to exact versions, adds dependency audit steps to CI, and implements a dependency review process for PRs that modify lock files.
subtasks: Pin all critical dependencies in package.json files (packages/receipt-uploader, cli, app/dashboard) to exact versions: remove ^ prefix from @coral-xyz/anchor, @solana/web3.js, @solana/spl-token, @irys/sdk, and all wallet adapter packages
Pin Cargo.toml workspace dependencies to exact versions: anchor-lang, anchor-spl, solana-sdk, solana-client, spl-token — use '=X.Y.Z' format or workspace-level dependency management
Add a 'cargo audit' step to the CI pipeline (rust-lint job) that fails on CRITICAL or HIGH advisories
Add a 'bun audit' or equivalent npm audit step to the CI pipeline for all TypeScript packages

Add a GitHub Actions dependency review step using 'actions/dependency-review-action@v4' that runs on PRs modifying Cargo.lock or bun.lockb files and flags known-vulnerable or license-problematic dependencies
Document the dependency management policy in docs/security/dependency-policy.md: exact pinning rationale, update cadence, audit requirements
acceptance_criteria: All package.json files use exact versions (no ^, ~, or * ranges) for security-critical dependencies — verified by grep
Cargo.toml uses exact version pins for anchor-lang, solana-sdk, and spl-token crates
CI pipeline includes cargo audit step that would fail on CRITICAL/HIGH advisories (currently passes with no known CVEs)
CI pipeline includes JavaScript/TypeScript dependency audit step
PRs modifying lock files trigger dependency-review-action and flag vulnerable dependencies
docs/security/dependency-policy.md exists with pinning rationale and update procedures
